title: Introduction to HOIF: Higher-Order Influence Function Estimators for the ATE
text: HOIF package implements Higher-Order Influence Function (HOIF) estimators Average Treatment Effect (ATE) estimation causal inference. methodology extends doubly robust (DR) estimator—also known Augmented Inverse Propensity Weighted (AIPW) Double Machine Learning (DML) estimator—systematically accounting higher-order bias terms.

title: Introduction to HOIF: Higher-Order Influence Function Estimators for the ATE
text: HOIF methodology developed series seminal works James M. Robins collaborators: Robins et al. (2008): Higher order influence functions minimax estimation nonlinear functionals Robins et al. (2017): Minimax estimation functional structured high-dimensional model Liu et al. (2017): Semiparametric efficient empirical higher order influence function estimators Liu & Li (2023): New √n-consistent, numerically stable higher-order influence function estimators key idea represent bias standard DR estimator sum higher-order influence functions, can estimated used debias original estimator. computation higher-order U-statistics arise HOIF estimators delegated ustats package; algorithm computational complexity analyzed : Chen, Zhang & Liu (2025): computing complexity computing higher-order U-statistics, exactly

title: Introduction to HOIF: Higher-Order Influence Function Estimators for the ATE
text: Flexible basis expansion: Supports B-splines, Fourier basis, raw covariates Multiple inversion methods: Direct, nonlinear shrinkage, corpcor regularization Sample splitting support: Optional K-fold cross-fitting estimation inverse weighted Gram matrix computation U-statistics (eHOIF; see Sample Splitting section) Dual computational backends: Fast Python backend via ustats package, pure R fallback order Python backend (pure R mode supports orders 6)

title: Introduction to HOIF: Higher-Order Influence Function Estimators for the ATE
text: Let ψ̂aAIPW\widehat{\psi}_a^{\mathrm{AIPW}} denote standard first-order doubly robust (AIPW/DML) estimator ψ=E[Y()]\psi_a = E[Y()], ATÊAIPW=ψ̂1AIPW−ψ̂0AIPW\widehat{\mathrm{ATE}}^{\mathrm{AIPW}} = \widehat{\psi}_1^{\mathrm{AIPW}} - \widehat{\psi}_0^{\mathrm{AIPW}}. Conditional estimated nuisance functions, estimator biased; HOIF estimates estimable part bias. treatment arm ∈{0,1}\\{0,1\}, mm-th order HOIF bias-correction term ℍ𝕆𝕀𝔽ma(Ω̂)=∑j=2m𝕀𝔽ja(Ω̂)=∑=2m∑j=2i(−2i−j)𝕌ja(Ω̂), \mathbb{HOIF}^a_m(\hat{\Omega}^) = \sum_{j=2}^m \mathbb{}^a_j(\hat{\Omega}^) = \sum_{=2}^m \sum_{j=2}^{} \binom{-2}{-j} \mathbb{U}^a_j(\hat{\Omega}^), corresponding correction ATE difference: 𝔹ℂmATE=ℍ𝕆𝕀𝔽m1(Ω̂1)−ℍ𝕆𝕀𝔽m0(Ω̂0). \mathbb{BC}^{\mathrm{ATE}}_m = \mathbb{HOIF}^1_m(\hat{\Omega}^1) - \mathbb{HOIF}^0_m(\hat{\Omega}^0). 𝔹ℂmATE\mathbb{BC}^{\mathrm{ATE}}_m mm-th order HOIF estimator estimable bias AIPW estimator ATE — estimator ATE. sign convention adding AIPW estimate removes bias: ATÊm=ATÊAIPW+𝔹ℂmATE. \widehat{\mathrm{ATE}}_m = \widehat{\mathrm{ATE}}^{\mathrm{AIPW}} + \mathbb{BC}^{\mathrm{ATE}}_m . output hoif_ate(), components HOIF1, HOIF0 ATE correspond ℍ𝕆𝕀𝔽m1\mathbb{HOIF}^1_m, ℍ𝕆𝕀𝔽m0\mathbb{HOIF}^0_m 𝔹ℂmATE\mathbb{BC}^{\mathrm{ATE}}_m m=2,…m = 2, \ldots (component name ATE kept backward compatibility — holds ATE bias correction, ATE ).

title: Introduction to HOIF: Higher-Order Influence Function Estimators for the ATE
text: core computational building block U-statistic: 𝕌ma(Ω̂)=(−1)m(n−m)!n!∑(i1,…,im):i1≠⋯≠imri1a∏s=1m−1{Zis⊤Ω̂asis+1aZis+1}Rima \mathbb{U}^a_m(\hat{\Omega}^) = (-1)^m \frac{(n-m)!}{n!} \sum_{\substack{(i_1, \ldots, i_m) : \\ i_1 \neq \cdots \neq i_m}} r^a_{i_1} \prod_{s=1}^{m-1} \{Z_{i_s}^\top \hat{\Omega}^s^a_{i_{s+1}} Z_{i_{s+1}}\} R^a_{i_m} : sia=Aia(1−Ai)1−^a_i = A_i^(1-A_i)^{1-} treatment indicator ria=1−sia/π(Xi)r^a_i = 1 - s^a_i / \pi^(X_i) inverse propensity weight residual Ria=Yi−μ̂(,Xi)R^a_i = Y_i - \hat{\mu}(, X_i) outcome residual ZiZ_i transformed covariates Ω̂\hat{\Omega}^inverse weighted Gram matrix full algorithmic workflow, mathematical formulas, parameters documented PDF shipped package (system.file("extdoc", "HOIF.pdf", package = "HOIF"), also available GitHub).

title: Introduction to HOIF: Higher-Order Influence Function Estimators for the ATE
text: Conceptually, HOIF estimation involves three separate estimation tasks, ideally use , independent part data: nuisance functions: estimating μ̂(1,X)\hat{\mu}(1, X), μ̂(0,X)\hat{\mu}(0, X) π̂(X)\hat{\pi}(X); inverse weighted Gram matrix Ω̂\hat{\Omega}^; higher-order U-statistics built Ω̂\hat{\Omega}^. package deliberately implement task 1: hoif_ate() takes predicted values mu1, mu0, pi inputs, (sample) nuisance functions estimated entirely user. Accordingly, full three-way cross-fitting performed package; sample_split argument controls split tasks 2 3: sample_split = TRUE (eHOIF): sample passed hoif_ate() split n_folds folds (I1,…,IK)(I_1, \ldots, I_K). fold jj, Ω̂\hat{\Omega}^estimated observations IjI_j, U-statistics computed observations IjI_j, results averaged across folds. sample_split = FALSE (sHOIF): Ω̂\hat{\Omega}^U-statistics computed sample, without distinction. Quick Start example follows structure: nuisance functions fitted one half data, hoif_ate() run half.

title: Introduction to HOIF: Higher-Order Influence Function Estimators for the ATE
code: # From CRAN (once accepted): # install.packages("HOIF")  # Development version from GitHub (also installs the ustats R package): # install.packages("devtools") devtools::install_github("cxy0714/HOIF")

title: Introduction to HOIF: Higher-Order Influence Function Estimators for the ATE
text: default backend computes higher-order U-statistics Python via ustats package. Python dependencies (u-stats, numpy, torch) provisioned automatically: reticulate (>= 1.41) installed, first call needs Python (e.g. first hoif_ate() call) downloads private Python together required packages cached environment reuses later sessions. manual setup needed cases: prefer persistent, dedicated environment, want control PyTorch installed (CPU-build vs. CUDA), use helpers ustats: can also point reticulate Python/conda environment (u-stats installed via pip install u-stats) Python initializes: See vignette("ustats", package = "ustats") complete installation guide troubleshooting tips.
code: library(HOIF) # First call provisions the Python environment automatically: # results <- hoif_ate(...) ustats::setup_ustats()             # CPU-only PyTorch (small download) ustats::setup_ustats(gpu = TRUE)   # default PyPI PyTorch (CUDA on Linux) ustats::check_ustats_setup()       # verify the environment reticulate::use_condaenv("your_env_name", required = TRUE) # or set the RETICULATE_PYTHON environment variable

title: Introduction to HOIF: Higher-Order Influence Function Estimators for the ATE
text: Set pure_R_code = TRUE hoif_ate(): U-statistics computed pure R implementation (orders 6) Python runtime required.

title: Introduction to HOIF: Higher-Order Influence Function Estimators for the ATE
text: Let’s demonstrate recommended workflow simulated example, following three-task structure described Sample Splitting section : nuisance functions fitted one half data (nuisance sample), hoif_ate() run half (estimation sample), within estimation sample compute eHOIF sHOIF estimators. make role HOIF correction visible, nuisance models deliberately misspecified (use 3 10 confounders), first-order AIPW estimator carries clear bias — HOIF terms estimate remove.

title: Introduction to HOIF: Higher-Order Influence Function Estimators for the ATE
code:
library(HOIF)  set.seed(123) n <- 2000 p <- 10  # Covariates (all of them are confounders) X <- matrix(rnorm(n * p), ncol = p)  # True propensity score and outcome regressions: linear, loading on # ALL covariates beta_pi <- c(0.3, -0.2, 0.2, rep(0.25, 7)) beta1   <- c(0.5,  0.4, 0.3, rep(0.4, 7)) beta0   <- c(0.3,  0.2, 0.1, rep(0.3, 7))  true_pi <- plogis(as.vector(X %*% beta_pi)) A <- rbinom(n, 1, true_pi)  mu1_true <- as.vector(1 + X %*% beta1) mu0_true <- as.vector(X %*% beta0) Y <- A * mu1_true + (1 - A) * mu0_true + rnorm(n, 0, 0.2)  # True targets psi1_true <- mean(mu1_true) psi0_true <- mean(mu0_true) true_ate  <- psi1_true - psi0_true cat("True E[Y(1)]:", round(psi1_true, 4), "
") #> True E[Y(1)]: 0.9799 cat("True E[Y(0)]:", round(psi0_true, 4), "
") #> True E[Y(0)]: -0.016 cat("True ATE:    ", round(true_ate, 4), "
") #> True ATE:     0.9958

title: Introduction to HOIF: Higher-Order Influence Function Estimators for the ATE
text: nuisance sample used fit nuisance functions; estimation sample used hoif_ate(). keeps nuisance predictions independent sample HOIF estimator computed.
code: idx_nuis <- sample(n, n / 2) idx_est  <- setdiff(seq_len(n), idx_nuis)  X_nuis <- X[idx_nuis, , drop = FALSE] A_nuis <- A[idx_nuis] Y_nuis <- Y[idx_nuis]  X_est <- X[idx_est, , drop = FALSE] A_est <- A[idx_est] Y_est <- Y[idx_est]

what: Estimate (Misspecified) Nuisance Functions on the Nuisance Sample
title: Introduction to HOIF: Higher-Order Influence Function Estimators for the ATE
text: package consumes predicted values nuisance functions; practice, flexible machine learning method can used produce . , mimic misspecification, working models use first 3 covariates ignore remaining confounders:
code: S <- 1:3  # the working models ignore covariates 4..10  # Outcome regressions, fitted on the nuisance sample only fit_mu1 <- lm(Y_nuis ~ X_nuis[, S], subset = A_nuis == 1) fit_mu0 <- lm(Y_nuis ~ X_nuis[, S], subset = A_nuis == 0)  # Propensity score, fitted on the nuisance sample only fit_pi <- glm(A_nuis ~ X_nuis[, S], family = binomial)  # Predict all nuisance functions on the estimation sample mu1_hat <- as.vector(cbind(1, X_est[, S]) %*% coef(fit_mu1)) mu0_hat <- as.vector(cbind(1, X_est[, S]) %*% coef(fit_mu0)) pi_hat  <- as.vector(plogis(cbind(1, X_est[, S]) %*% coef(fit_pi)))  # Ensure propensity scores are bounded away from 0 and 1 pi_hat <- pmax(pmin(pi_hat, 0.95), 0.05)

what: The First-Order AIPW Estimator and its Bias
title: Introduction to HOIF: Higher-Order Influence Function Estimators for the ATE
text: HOIF terms estimate estimable bias standard first-order doubly robust (AIPW/DML) estimator, let us compute estimator first, together actual error: misspecified nuisance models, AIPW estimator misses true ATE 0.45 — bias amount data remove. now estimate bias HOIF.
code: psi1_aipw <- mean(mu1_hat + A_est / pi_hat * (Y_est - mu1_hat)) psi0_aipw <- mean(mu0_hat + (1 - A_est) / (1 - pi_hat) * (Y_est - mu0_hat)) ate_aipw  <- psi1_aipw - psi0_aipw  cat("AIPW estimates: E[Y(1)] =", round(psi1_aipw, 4),     "  E[Y(0)] =", round(psi0_aipw, 4),     "  ATE =", round(ate_aipw, 4), "\n") #> AIPW estimates: E[Y(1)] = 1.1749   E[Y(0)] = -0.2676   ATE = 1.4425 cat("AIPW errors:    E[Y(1)] =", round(psi1_aipw - psi1_true, 4),     "  E[Y(0)] =", round(psi0_aipw - psi0_true, 4),     "  ATE =", round(ate_aipw - true_ate, 4), "\n") #> AIPW errors:    E[Y(1)] = 0.195   E[Y(0)] = -0.2516   ATE = 0.4466

what: Compute the eHOIF Estimator (with sample splitting)
title: Introduction to HOIF: Higher-Order Influence Function Estimators for the ATE
text: Within estimation sample, hoif_ate() cross-fits inverse weighted Gram matrix U-statistics n_folds folds:
code: results_ehoif <- hoif_ate(   X = X_est,   A = A_est,   Y = Y_est,   mu1 = mu1_hat,   mu0 = mu0_hat,   pi = pi_hat,   transform_method = "none",  # Use raw covariates   inverse_method = "direct",   m = 7,                      # Compute up to 7th order   sample_split = TRUE,   n_folds = 2,   seed = 42,   backend = "torch"           # Use Python backend )  print(results_ehoif) #> HOIF Estimators for Average Treatment Effect #> ============================================= #> #> Higher-order correction terms by order: #>   Order     ATE   HOIF1  HOIF0 #> 1     2 -0.4621 -0.2615 0.2006 #> 2     3 -0.4050 -0.2341 0.1709 #> 3     4 -0.4335 -0.2480 0.1855 #> 4     5 -0.4272 -0.2443 0.1829 #> 5     6 -0.4289 -0.2456 0.1833 #> 6     7 -0.4293 -0.2455 0.1838 #> #> Estimated AIPW bias correction for the ATE (highest order): -0.4293 #> (add this value to the first-order AIPW/DR estimate of the ATE to debias it)

what: Compute the sHOIF Estimator (without sample splitting)
title: Introduction to HOIF: Higher-Order Influence Function Estimators for the ATE
text: Still using independent nuisance predictions ; split Gram matrix U-statistics dropped:
code: results_shoif <- hoif_ate(   X = X_est,   A = A_est,   Y = Y_est,   mu1 = mu1_hat,   mu0 = mu0_hat,   pi = pi_hat,   transform_method = "none",   inverse_method = "direct",   m = 7,   sample_split = FALSE,   backend = "torch" )  print(results_shoif) #> HOIF Estimators for Average Treatment Effect #> ============================================= #> #> Higher-order correction terms by order: #>   Order     ATE   HOIF1  HOIF0 #> 1     2 -0.4318 -0.2496 0.1821 #> 2     3 -0.4458 -0.2568 0.1890 #> 3     4 -0.4373 -0.2520 0.1853 #> 4     5 -0.4362 -0.2515 0.1848 #> 5     6 -0.4366 -0.2517 0.1849 #> 6     7 -0.4367 -0.2517 0.1849 #> #> Estimated AIPW bias correction for the ATE (highest order): -0.4367 #> (add this value to the first-order AIPW/DR estimate of the ATE to debias it)

title: Introduction to HOIF: Higher-Order Influence Function Estimators for the ATE
text: reported HOIF1/HOIF0/ATE values higher-order influence function terms orders 2 mm — point estimates E[Y(1)]E[Y(1)], E[Y(0)]E[Y(0)] ATE. estimate estimable bias AIPW estimator, sign convention adding AIPW estimates removes : HOIF correction removes essentially AIPW bias ATE (error 0.4466→0.4466 \rightarrow 0.010.01) bulk treatment arm.
code: psi1_ehoif <- psi1_aipw + tail(results_ehoif$HOIF1, 1) psi0_ehoif <- psi0_aipw + tail(results_ehoif$HOIF0, 1) psi1_shoif <- psi1_aipw + tail(results_shoif$HOIF1, 1) psi0_shoif <- psi0_aipw + tail(results_shoif$HOIF0, 1)  comparison <- data.frame(   row.names = c("E[Y(1)]", "E[Y(0)]", "ATE"),   Truth = c(psi1_true, psi0_true, true_ate),   AIPW  = c(psi1_aipw, psi0_aipw, ate_aipw),   eHOIF = c(psi1_ehoif, psi0_ehoif, psi1_ehoif - psi0_ehoif),   sHOIF = c(psi1_shoif, psi0_shoif, psi1_shoif - psi0_shoif) ) round(comparison, 4) #>           Truth    AIPW   eHOIF   sHOIF #> E[Y(1)]  0.9799  1.1749  0.9294  0.9232 #> E[Y(0)] -0.0160 -0.2676 -0.0838 -0.0826 #> ATE      0.9958  1.4425  1.0132  1.0058  # Errors relative to the truth round(sweep(comparison[, -1], 1, comparison$Truth, "-"), 4) #>            AIPW   eHOIF   sHOIF #> E[Y(1)]  0.1950 -0.0505 -0.0567 #> E[Y(0)] -0.2516 -0.0678 -0.0666 #> ATE      0.4466  0.0174  0.0100

title: Introduction to HOIF: Higher-Order Influence Function Estimators for the ATE
code: plot(results_ehoif)

title: Introduction to HOIF: Higher-Order Influence Function Estimators for the ATE
text: primary interface computing HOIF estimators.

title: Introduction to HOIF: Higher-Order Influence Function Estimators for the ATE
text: X: Covariate matrix (n×pn \times p) : Treatment vector (0 1) Y: Outcome vector mu1, mu0: Predicted outcomes treatment control pi: Predicted propensity scores "none": transformation (use raw covariates) "splines": B-splines basis expansion "fourier": Fourier basis expansion basis_dim: Number basis functions (required using splines Fourier) "direct": Cholesky-based inversion (Moore-Penrose fallback) "nlshrink": Nonlinear shrinkage (Ledoit-Wolf) "corpcor": Shrinkage via corpcor package m: Maximum order (order Python backend, 2-6 pure R) sample_split: Logical; whether cross-fit Gram matrix U-statistics (eHOIF vs sHOIF, see Sample Splitting section) n_folds: Number folds sample splitting backend: "torch" (default) "numpy" Python backend dtype: Numeric precision Python backend ("float32", "float64", NULL automatic selection) pure_R_code: Use pure R implementation (limited order 6) seed: Random seed reproducibility

title: Introduction to HOIF: Higher-Order Influence Function Estimators for the ATE
text: list class "hoif_ate" containing: ATE: Vector ATE estimates orders 2 m HOIF1: Vector treatment arm HOIF estimates HOIF0: Vector control arm HOIF estimates IIFF1: Vector treatment arm incremental influence function terms IIFF0: Vector control arm incremental influence function terms orders: Vector orders (2:m) convergence_data: Data frame plotting

title: Introduction to HOIF: Higher-Order Influence Function Estimators for the ATE
text: flexible modeling covariate relationships:
code: # B-splines basis results_splines <- hoif_ate(   X = X_est, A = A_est, Y = Y_est,   mu1 = mu1_hat, mu0 = mu0_hat, pi = pi_hat,   transform_method = "splines",   basis_dim = 5,        # 5 basis functions per covariate   degree = 3,           # Cubic splines   m = 5,   sample_split = FALSE )  # Fourier basis results_fourier <- hoif_ate(   X = X_est, A = A_est, Y = Y_est,   mu1 = mu1_hat, mu0 = mu0_hat, pi = pi_hat,   transform_method = "fourier",   basis_dim = 4,        # 4 Fourier components per covariate   period = 1,   m = 5,   sample_split = FALSE )

title: Introduction to HOIF: Higher-Order Influence Function Estimators for the ATE
text: cross-fit estimation inverse weighted Gram matrix computation U-statistics — eHOIF estimator (see Sample Splitting section Mathematical Background exactly split):
code: results_cf <- hoif_ate(   X = X_est, A = A_est, Y = Y_est,   mu1 = mu1_hat, mu0 = mu0_hat, pi = pi_hat,   transform_method = "none",   m = 5,   sample_split = TRUE,   n_folds = 5,          # 5-fold cross-fitting   seed = 42 )

title: Introduction to HOIF: Higher-Order Influence Function Estimators for the ATE
text: high-dimensional settings facing numerical instability:
code: # Nonlinear shrinkage results_nlshrink <- hoif_ate(   X = X_est, A = A_est, Y = Y_est,   mu1 = mu1_hat, mu0 = mu0_hat, pi = pi_hat,   inverse_method = "nlshrink",   m = 5 )  # corpcor shrinkage results_corpcor <- hoif_ate(   X = X_est, A = A_est, Y = Y_est,   mu1 = mu1_hat, mu0 = mu0_hat, pi = pi_hat,   inverse_method = "corpcor",   m = 5 )

title: Introduction to HOIF: Higher-Order Influence Function Estimators for the ATE
text: Python environment available smaller problems:
code: results_pure_r <- hoif_ate(   X = X_est, A = A_est, Y = Y_est,   mu1 = mu1_hat, mu0 = mu0_hat, pi = pi_hat,   pure_R_code = TRUE,   m = 6  # Maximum 6 for pure R )

title: Introduction to HOIF: Higher-Order Influence Function Estimators for the ATE
text: default backend uses ustats R package, interface Python package u-stats, efficient U-statistic computation: Supports PyTorch NumPy backends (GPU acceleration CUDA-enabled PyTorch) Handles arbitrary order U-statistics Optimized memory speed via Einstein summation HOIF estimators ATE, key takeaway Chen, Zhang & Liu (2025) : order m≤7m \le 7, computational complexity O(n3+nk2+k3+n2k)O(n^3 + nk^2 + k^3 + n^2 k) order m>7m > 7, computational complexity exceeds O(n4+nk2+k3+n2k)O(n^4 + nk^2 + k^3 + n^2 k) nn sample size kk dimension transformed covariates.

title: Introduction to HOIF: Higher-Order Influence Function Estimators for the ATE
text: pure R fallback (pure_R_code = TRUE): Implements orders 2-6 using recursive matrix operations Uses SMUT package fast matrix multiplication Removes diagonal contributions ensure proper degenerate U-statistics Suitable smaller datasets Python unavailable

title: Introduction to HOIF: Higher-Order Influence Function Estimators for the ATE
text: Order: Higher orders increase computation time Basis dimension: Larger basis increases memory computation Cross-fitting: Multiplies computation number folds Precision: CUDA GPU Python backend defaults float32; pass dtype = "float64" maximum numerical precision

title: Introduction to HOIF: Higher-Order Influence Function Estimators for the ATE
text: “none”: Use covariates provided (e.g. already constructed basis) “splines”: Good smooth nonlinear relationships “fourier”: Suitable periodic patterns high-frequency features

title: Introduction to HOIF: Higher-Order Influence Function Estimators for the ATE
text: orders m≤7m \le 7 computational complexity exceed ordinary matrix computations, O(n3+nk2+k3+n2k)O(n^3 + nk^2 + k^3 + n^2 k) (Chen, Zhang & Liu, 2025), going order 7 cheap. practical default therefore compute orders m=7m = 7 (package default) inspect convergence plot. Beyond order 7 complexity exceeds O(n4+nk2+k3+n2k)O(n^4 + nk^2 + k^3 + n^2 k), expect substantially longer run times.

title: Introduction to HOIF: Higher-Order Influence Function Estimators for the ATE
text: backend = "torch" CUDA-enabled PyTorch (e.g. installed via ustats::setup_ustats(gpu = TRUE)), U-statistics computed GPU automatically — far effective speedup large nn kk. GPU default precision float32; pass dtype = "float64" need maximum numerical precision.

title: Introduction to HOIF: Higher-Order Influence Function Estimators for the ATE
text: See Sample Splitting section Mathematical Background: package cross-fits inverse weighted Gram matrix U-statistics (sample_split = TRUE, eHOIF). nuisance functions inputs — estimating , ideally separate, independent sample, user’s responsibility (demonstrated Quick Start example).

title: Introduction to HOIF: Higher-Order Influence Function Estimators for the ATE
text: Verify Python environment used ustats: environment repaired easily, switch pure R implementation:
code: ustats::check_ustats_setup() results <- hoif_ate(   X = X_est, A = A_est, Y = Y_est,   mu1 = mu1_hat, mu0 = mu0_hat, pi = pi_hat,   pure_R_code = TRUE,   m = 6  # Limited to order 6 )

title: Introduction to HOIF: Higher-Order Influence Function Estimators for the ATE
text: see warnings matrix inversion:
code: # Try shrinkage methods results <- hoif_ate(   X = X_est, A = A_est, Y = Y_est,   mu1 = mu1_hat, mu0 = mu0_hat, pi = pi_hat,   inverse_method = "nlshrink"  # or "corpcor" )

title: Introduction to HOIF: Higher-Order Influence Function Estimators for the ATE
text: Always trim propensity scores avoid numerical issues:
code: # Trim to [0.05, 0.95] pi_hat <- pmax(pmin(pi_hat, 0.95), 0.05)

title: Introduction to HOIF: Higher-Order Influence Function Estimators for the ATE

title: Higher-Order Influence Function Estimators for the Average Treatment Effect
text: HOIF R package implementation Higher-Order Influence Function (HOIF) estimators Average Treatment Effect (ATE). methodology based series foundational works James M. Robins collaborators [1–4]. overall algorithmic workflow, mathematical formulas, parameters HOIF illustrated HOIF.pdf (GitHub) (installation: system.file("extdoc", "HOIF.pdf", package = "HOIF")) package vignette — can used references.

title: Higher-Order Influence Function Estimators for the Average Treatment Effect
text: core computational component HOIF evaluation higher-order U-statistics. developed general algorithm computing U-statistics using powerful Python functions numpy.einsum torch.einsum. built Python package, u-stats (source: U-Statistics-python), along R interface provided ustats. Using ustats, developed R package HOIF estimation ATE. also analyze computational complexity using graph-theoretic tools dedicated paper focused exact computation higher-order U-statistics [5]. HOIF estimators ATE, key takeaway : order m≤7m \le 7, computational complexity O(n3+nk2+k3+n2k)O(n^3 + nk^2 + k^3 + n^2 k) order m>7m > 7, computational complexity exceeds O(n4+nk2+k3+n2k)O(n^4 + nk^2 + k^3 + n^2 k) , nn sample size kk user-defined dimension transformed covariates XX. details computing higher-order U-statistics arising HOIF, see Section 4.1 [5].

title: Higher-Order Influence Function Estimators for the Average Treatment Effect
text: code, note sample splitting. Conceptually, HOIF estimation involves three estimation tasks, ideally uses , independent part data: estimating nuisance functions μ̂(1,X),μ̂(0,X),π̂(X)\hat{\mu}(1, X), \hat{\mu}(0, X), \hat{\pi}(X); estimating inverse weighted Gram matrix Ω̂\hat{\Omega}^; computing higher-order U-statistics. package implement task 1: hoif_ate() takes predicted values (mu1, mu0, pi) inputs, overall three-way cross-fitting left user. sample_split argument controls split tasks 2 3: sample_split = TRUE (eHOIF): cross-fits Gram matrix U-statistics n_folds folds averages results; sample_split = FALSE (sHOIF): computes sample, without distinction. example shows recommended workflow. data first split half: nuisance functions fitted one half (task 1) hoif_ate() run half (tasks 2–3), nuisance predictions independent estimation sample. Within estimation half, eHOIF additionally cross-fits Gram matrix U-statistics, sHOIF uses estimation half . comprehensive example can found test_manual/manual_test.R.
code:
library(HOIF)  set.seed(123)  n <- 2000   # Total sample size p <- 50     # Number of covariates  # Covariates X <- matrix(rnorm(n * p), ncol = p)  # Binary treatment assignment (randomized) A <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.5)  # True coefficient vector (normalized) beta <- runif(p) beta <- beta / sqrt(as.numeric(crossprod(beta)))  # Outcome: partially linear model Y <- as.numeric(A + X %*% beta + rnorm(n, 0, 0.1))   ### Step 1: Split the sample --------------------------------------------- ### One half (the "nuisance sample") is used only to fit the nuisance ### regressions; the other half (the "estimation sample") is used only by ### hoif_ate(). This keeps the nuisance predictions independent of the ### sample on which the HOIF estimator is computed.  idx_nuis <- sample(n, n / 2) idx_est  <- setdiff(seq_len(n), idx_nuis)  X_nuis <- X[idx_nuis, , drop = FALSE] A_nuis <- A[idx_nuis] Y_nuis <- Y[idx_nuis]  X_est <- X[idx_est, , drop = FALSE] A_est <- A[idx_est] Y_est <- Y[idx_est]   ### Step 2: Estimate nuisance outcome regressions on the nuisance sample ### and predict them on the estimation sample (here we omit the estimation ### of the propensity score by using the known randomization probability)  # - mu1(X) = E[Y | A = 1, X] # - mu0(X) = E[Y | A = 0, X]  library(glmnet)  cv_fit1 <- cv.glmnet(X_nuis[A_nuis == 1, , drop = FALSE],                      Y_nuis[A_nuis == 1], alpha = 1) cv_fit0 <- cv.glmnet(X_nuis[A_nuis == 0, , drop = FALSE],                      Y_nuis[A_nuis == 0], alpha = 1)  mu1 <- as.vector(predict(cv_fit1, newx = X_est, s = "lambda.min")) mu0 <- as.vector(predict(cv_fit0, newx = X_est, s = "lambda.min"))  # Known propensity score (randomized treatment) pi <- rep(0.5, length(idx_est))   ### Step 3: eHOIF -- cross-fit the Gram matrix vs the U-statistics -------- ### Within the estimation sample, hoif_ate() splits the data into n_folds ### folds: for each fold, the inverse weighted Gram matrix is estimated on ### the other folds, the U-statistics are computed on the held-out fold, ### and the results are averaged.  m <- 7 n_folds <- 2  results_split <- hoif_ate(   X_est, A_est, Y_est,   mu1 = mu1,   mu0 = mu0,   pi = pi,   transform_method = "none",   m = m,   sample_split = TRUE,   n_folds = n_folds,   seed = 123,   backend = "torch" )  cat("
Results (eHOIF, with sample splitting):
") print(results_split) plot(results_split)   ### Step 4: sHOIF -- Gram matrix and U-statistics on the same sample ------ ### Still uses the independent nuisance predictions from Step 2; only the ### split between the Gram matrix and the U-statistics is dropped.  results_no_split <- hoif_ate(   X_est, A_est, Y_est,   mu1 = mu1,   mu0 = mu0,   pi = pi,   transform_method = "none",   m = m,   sample_split = FALSE,   backend = "torch" )  cat("
Results (sHOIF, without sample splitting):
") print(results_no_split) plot(results_no_split)

title: Higher-Order Influence Function Estimators for the Average Treatment Effect
text: 1 Robins, J., Li, L., Tchetgen Tchetgen, E., & van der Vaart, . (2008). Higher order influence functions minimax estimation nonlinear functionals. Probability Statistics: Essays Honor David . Freedman, 335–421. 2 Robins, J. M., Li, L., Mukherjee, R., Tchetgen Tchetgen, E., & van der Vaart, . (2017). Minimax estimation functional structured high-dimensional model. Annals Statistics, 45(5), 1951–1987. 3 Liu, L., Mukherjee, R., Newey, W. K., & Robins, J. M. (2017). Semiparametric efficient empirical higher order influence function estimators. arXiv:1705.07577. 4 Liu, L., & Li, C. (2023). New √n-consistent, numerically stable higher-order influence function estimators. arXiv:2302.08097. 5 Chen, X., Zhang, R., & Liu, L. (2025). computing complexity computing higher-order U-statistics, exactly. arXiv:2508.12627.

title: Compute U-statistics HOIF-type from Order 2 to 6 in pure R code. — calculate_u_statistics_pure_r_six
text: named list containing numeric U-statistic estimates: U2 Second-order U-statistic U3 Third-order U-statistic U4 Fourth-order U-statistic U5 Fifth-order U-statistic U6 Sixth-order U-statistic

title: Compute U-statistics HOIF-type from Order 2 to 6 in pure R code. — calculate_u_statistics_pure_r_six
text: Internally, function constructs kernel matrices orders 2 6 using recursive matrix operations removes diagonal contributions ensure degenerate U-statistics. diagonal elements intermediate kernel matrices removed avoid self-interactions. Matrix multiplications performed via `eigenMapMatMult()` element-wise products via `hadamard()`. exact formula output : $$ \mathbb{U}_{n,m} = \frac{1}{\binom{n}{m} m!} \sum_{i_1 \ne \cdots \ne i_m} Vector_1[i_1] \cdot A1[i_1,i_2] \cdot A1[i_2,i_3] \cdots A1[i_{m-1},i_{m}] \cdot Vector_2[i_{m}] $$

title: Compute HOIF Estimators for ATE — compute_hoif_estimators
text: residuals list containing computed residuals: `R1`, `r1`, `R0`, `r0`. B_matrices list containing projection-like basis matrices: `B1` `B0`. m Integer. maximum order HOIF estimator. backend Character. computation backend used ustat; either "torch" (default) "numpy". PyTorch available, 'ustats' falls back "numpy" warning. pure_R_code Logical. Whether use native R implementation. serves fallback Python environment used 'ustats' (via 'reticulate') available. Note: pure R implementation supports 6th order (m = 6). dtype Optional character string passed ustat controlling numeric precision Python backend: "float32" "float64". default NULL selects precision automatically (float32 CUDA GPU, float64 otherwise). Ignored pure_R_code = TRUE.

title: Compute HOIF Estimators for ATE — compute_hoif_estimators

title: Compute HOIF Estimators for ATE — compute_hoif_estimators
code: # Pure R example (no Python required), up to order 6 n <- 100 Z <- cbind(1, rnorm(n)) A <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.5) Y <- A + Z[, 2] + rnorm(n) residuals <- compute_residuals(A, Y, mu1 = 1 + Z[, 2], mu0 = Z[, 2],                                pi = rep(0.5, n)) Omega <- compute_gram_inverse(Z, A) B <- compute_basis_matrix(Z, A, Omega$Omega1, Omega$Omega0) est <- compute_hoif_estimators(residuals, B, m = 6, pure_R_code = TRUE) est$ATE #> [1] -0.04685879 -0.05019283 -0.04800393 -0.04703972 -0.04698073  if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Python backend (requires the 'ustats' Python dependencies), any order est <- compute_hoif_estimators(residuals, B, m = 7, backend = "torch") } # }

what: Main function: HOIF estimators for ATE with optional sample splitting — hoif_ate
title: Main function: HOIF estimators for ATE with optional sample splitting — hoif_ate
text: Computes higher-order influence function terms orders 2 m, estimate estimable bias standard first-order doubly robust (AIPW) estimator ATE used debias .

title: Main function: HOIF estimators for ATE with optional sample splitting — hoif_ate
code: hoif_ate(   X,   A,   Y,   mu1,   mu0,   pi,   transform_method = "none",   basis_dim = NULL,   inverse_method = "direct",   m = 7,   sample_split = FALSE,   n_folds = 2,   backend = "torch",   seed = 42,   pure_R_code = FALSE,   dtype = NULL,   ... )

title: Main function: HOIF estimators for ATE with optional sample splitting — hoif_ate
text: X Covariate matrix (n x p) Treatment vector (n x 1) Y Outcome vector (n x 1) mu1 Predicted outcomes treatment (predictions supplied user, ideally estimated separate, independent sample) mu0 Predicted outcomes control (see `mu1`) pi Predicted propensity scores (see `mu1`) transform_method Character: method transform covariates constructing basis functions. - "splines": use basis splines expansion - "fourier": use Fourier basis expansion - "none": transformation (use raw covariates) basis_dim Integer: number basis functions generate using "splines" "fourier" transformations. Higher values provide flexible approximations may increase variance. inverse_method Character: regularization method Gram matrix inversion. - "direct": Cholesky-based inversion (falls back Moore-Penrose   inverse MASS Gram matrix positive definite) - "nlshrink": nonlinear shrinkage estimator (Ledoit-Wolf type) - "corpcor": shrinkage via corpcor package (high-dimensional settings) m Maximum order HOIF (6 pure_R_code = TRUE) sample_split Logical: whether cross-fit inverse weighted Gram matrix U-statistics. `TRUE` (eHOIF case), sample split `n_folds` folds; fold, Gram matrix estimated remaining folds, U-statistics computed fold, results averaged across folds. `FALSE` (sHOIF case), computed sample, without distinction. Note cross-fitting nuisance functions, whose predictions supplied via `mu1`, `mu0`, `pi`. n_folds Number folds sample splitting (used) backend Character: computation backend used ustat; "torch" (default) "numpy". Ignored pure_R_code = TRUE. seed Random seed reproducibility (sample splitting) pure_R_code Logical: `TRUE`, higher-order U-statistics computed pure R implementation (Python required), supports orders m = 6. `FALSE` (default), computed 'ustats' package, whose Python dependencies provisioned automatically first use (see package README). dtype Optional character string ("float32" "float64") controlling numeric precision Python backend; `NULL` (default) selects precision automatically. Passed ustat; ignored pure_R_code = TRUE. ... Additional arguments passed transform_covariates

title: Main function: HOIF estimators for ATE with optional sample splitting — hoif_ate
text: object class "hoif_ate": list components ATE ATE estimates orders 2 m HOIF1, HOIF0 HOIF estimates treated/control arm IIFF1, IIFF0 Incremental influence function terms     treated/control arm orders orders 2 m convergence_data Data frame ATE estimate per order

title: Main function: HOIF estimators for ATE with optional sample splitting — hoif_ate
text: Conceptually, HOIF estimation involves three estimation tasks, ideally uses , independent part data: (1) estimating nuisance functions mu(1, X), mu(0, X) pi(X); (2) estimating inverse weighted Gram matrix; (3) computing higher-order U-statistics. package implement task (1): `hoif_ate()` takes nuisance *predictions* `mu1`, `mu0` `pi` inputs, overall three-way cross-fitting left user. `sample_split` argument controls split tasks (2) (3); see description .

title: Main function: HOIF estimators for ATE with optional sample splitting — hoif_ate
code: # A small, self-contained example using the pure R backend set.seed(1) n <- 100 X <- matrix(rnorm(n), ncol = 1) A <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.5) Y <- as.numeric(A + X %*% 0.5 + rnorm(n, 0, 0.1)) mu1 <- as.numeric(1 + X %*% 0.5) mu0 <- as.numeric(X %*% 0.5) pi <- rep(0.5, n)  fit <- hoif_ate(X, A, Y, mu1 = mu1, mu0 = mu0, pi = pi,                 transform_method = "none", m = 6,                 pure_R_code = TRUE) print(fit) #> HOIF Estimators for Average Treatment Effect #> ============================================= #>  #> Higher-order correction terms by order: #>   Order    ATE  HOIF1 HOIF0 #> 1     2 -5e-04 -3e-04 1e-04 #> 2     3 -4e-04 -3e-04 2e-04 #> 3     4 -4e-04 -2e-04 2e-04 #> 4     5 -4e-04 -2e-04 2e-04 #> 5     6 -4e-04 -2e-04 2e-04 #>  #> Estimated AIPW bias correction for the ATE (highest order): -4e-04  #> (add this value to the first-order AIPW/DR estimate of the ATE to debias it)  if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Python backend (provisioned automatically on first use), order m = 7, # with 2-fold sample splitting fit <- hoif_ate(X, A, Y, mu1 = mu1, mu0 = mu0, pi = pi,                 transform_method = "none", m = 7,                 sample_split = TRUE, n_folds = 2, seed = 123,                 backend = "torch") print(fit) plot(fit) } # }

what: HOIF Estimators for Average Treatment Effect — transform_covariates
title: HOIF Estimators for Average Treatment Effect — transform_covariates
text: file implements Higher Order Influence Function (HOIF) estimators Average Treatment Effect (ATE) estimation nuisance functions.

title: HOIF Estimators for Average Treatment Effect — transform_covariates
code: transform_covariates(X, method = "splines", basis_dim, degree = 3, period = 1)

title: HOIF Estimators for Average Treatment Effect — transform_covariates
text: X Matrix covariates (n x p) method Character: "splines", "fourier", "none" basis_dim Integer: dimension basis expansion per covariate (ignored method = "none"; must least degree + 1 "splines" least 2 "fourier") degree Integer: degree B-splines (default 3; ignored method != "splines") period Numeric: period Fourier basis (default 1; ignored method != "fourier")

title: HOIF Estimators for Average Treatment Effect — transform_covariates
text: Matrix transformed covariates. method = "none" input   X returned unchanged (n x p); "splines" "fourier" basis   expansions covariates column-bound together   intercept column.

title: HOIF Estimators for Average Treatment Effect — transform_covariates
text: Xingyu Chen Transform covariates basis functions

title: HOIF Estimators for Average Treatment Effect — transform_covariates
code: X <- matrix(rnorm(40), nrow = 20, ncol = 2) Z_splines <- transform_covariates(X, method = "splines", basis_dim = 5) Z_fourier <- transform_covariates(X, method = "fourier", basis_dim = 4) Z_raw <- transform_covariates(X, method = "none") dim(Z_splines) #> [1] 20  9 dim(Z_fourier) #> [1] 20  9

title: HOIF 0.2.0
text: First CRAN submission.

title: HOIF 0.2.0
text: Python dependencies (u-stats, numpy, torch) ‘ustats’ backend now provisioned automatically first use (via reticulate >= 1.41 reticulate::py_require() declared ‘ustats’), users need manual setup. installation documentation (README, vignette) rewritten accordingly, covering three setup paths: automatic, ustats::setup_ustats() (CPU-PyTorch default, gpu = TRUE CUDA), bring---environment. hoif_ate() compute_hoif_estimators() gain dtype argument (“float32”/“float64”, default NULL = automatic) passed ustats::ustat() control numeric precision Python backend. Remotes: field package-level Config/reticulate block removed: ‘ustats’ now obtained CRAN declares Python requirements.

title: HOIF 0.2.0
text: transform_covariates() longer fails “object ‘k’ found” method = "splines" method = "fourier" (basis dimension argument renamed basis_dim without updating function body). now also validates basis_dim gives informative error missing small. Moore-Penrose fallback compute_gram_inverse() now checks ‘MASS’ installed (suggested package) gives informative error . print.hoif_ate() returns argument invisibly, customary print methods.

title: HOIF 0.2.0
text: exported functions now executable examples; examples tests need Python guarded package can checked without Python runtime (pure R backend exercised instead). Added tests/testthat.R runner (previously test suite run R CMD check) plus new pure R regression tests. vignette moved standard vignettes/ directory, code chunks longer evaluated build time (Python backend available build machines), references corrected. Startup message points users ustats::check_ustats_setup() pure_R_code = TRUE fallback.
